description:
Compliance scenario – simple regex-based log filtering.

  Context:
  A sample system log has been placed at  
  /home/user/audit/syslog_sample.log  

  You are acting as a compliance officer who must quickly pull out two
  kinds of security-relevant events:

    1. Every “Failed password …” message that indicates a failed SSH login
       (case-sensitive, it always begins with the exact phrase
       “Failed password” somewhere after the time-stamp).

    2. Every “Session opened for user root …” message that records a
       privileged root login being opened (case-sensitive, it always
       contains the exact phrase “Session opened for user root”).

  Required end-state of the system
  --------------------------------
  By the time you finish, exactly three new plain-text files must exist.
  All paths are absolute and must be created if they do not yet exist.

    a) /home/user/audit/extracted_failed.log  
       • Contains ONLY the unmodified log lines that matched rule 1.  
       • Preserve the original order they appeared in
         /home/user/audit/syslog_sample.log.  
       • The file must NOT contain blank lines or any extra text.

    b) /home/user/audit/root_sessions.log  
       • Contains ONLY the unmodified log lines that matched rule 2.  
       • Preserve their original order.  
       • No blank lines or extra text.

    c) /home/user/audit/audit_counts.txt  
       • Exactly two lines, each in KEY=VALUE format, no spaces:
           FAILED_LOGINS=<number_of_lines_in_extracted_failed.log>
           ROOT_SESSIONS=<number_of_lines_in_root_sessions.log>
       • Example of correct shape (numbers will vary):
           FAILED_LOGINS=12
           ROOT_SESSIONS=3
       • Use uppercase keys exactly as shown, newline-terminated.

  Validation notes:
  • “Failed password” and “Session opened for user root” must be matched
    using regular expressions (grep, awk, sed or similar), not manual copy
    & paste.  
  • The order of lines in the two output logs has to be identical to the
    source file.  
  • The counts in audit_counts.txt must accurately reflect the two output
    files (an automated test will compare them).  
  • Do NOT modify the original /home/user/audit/syslog_sample.log file.

  Hints (not commands):
  • grep -E with an anchored phrase like “Failed password” can do rule 1.  
  • Take advantage of redirection & pipelines to generate the three files
    with no intermediate temp files if you wish.

  Your goal is to produce those three files exactly as described so that
  an automated compliance check can pass.
truth:
Pre-existing artefacts before the agent starts:
  ----------------------------------------------
  1) A directory /home/user/audit already exists and is writable
     by the normal user.

  2) File /home/user/audit/syslog_sample.log exists with the
     following 13 lines (each ends with a newline character):

     Jan 12 14:35:02 server1 sshd[1024]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.25 port 54421 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:36:15 server1 sshd[1025]: Session opened for user root by (uid=0)
     Jan 12 14:37:20 server1 sshd[1025]: Session closed for user root
     Jan 12 14:40:55 server1 sshd[1030]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.26 port 54422 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:42:03 server1 sshd[1031]: Accepted password for user bob from 192.168.1.27 port 54423 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:42:04 server1 sshd[1031]: session opened for user bob by (uid=0)
     Jan 12 14:45:10 server1 sudo: pam_unix(sudo:session): session opened for user root by bob(uid=0)
     Jan 12 14:46:33 server1 sshd[1036]: Failed password for invalid user guest from 192.168.1.28 port 54424 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:48:57 server1 sshd[1040]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.29 port 54425 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:49:01 server1 sshd[1040]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.29 port 54425 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:50:12 server1 sshd[1045]: Session opened for user root by (uid=0)
     Jan 12 14:51:20 server1 sshd[1045]: Session closed for user root
     Jan 12 14:52:30 server1 sshd[1050]: Failed password for invalid user test from 192.168.1.30 port 54426 ssh2

  Expected artefacts **after** the agent completes the task:
  ----------------------------------------------------------
  A) /home/user/audit/extracted_failed.log  
     (6 lines, exactly the following lines in this order)

     Jan 12 14:35:02 server1 sshd[1024]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.25 port 54421 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:40:55 server1 sshd[1030]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.26 port 54422 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:46:33 server1 sshd[1036]: Failed password for invalid user guest from 192.168.1.28 port 54424 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:48:57 server1 sshd[1040]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.29 port 54425 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:49:01 server1 sshd[1040]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.29 port 54425 ssh2
     Jan 12 14:52:30 server1 sshd[1050]: Failed password for invalid user test from 192.168.1.30 port 54426 ssh2

  B) /home/user/audit/root_sessions.log  
     (2 lines, exactly the following lines in this order)

     Jan 12 14:36:15 server1 sshd[1025]: Session opened for user root by (uid=0)
     Jan 12 14:50:12 server1 sshd[1045]: Session opened for user root by (uid=0)

  C) /home/user/audit/audit_counts.txt  
     (exactly 2 lines, newline-terminated)

     FAILED_LOGINS=6
     ROOT_SESSIONS=2
